speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll two with Martin Kohn. Roll two, Martin Kohn. Roll two, Martin Kohn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you forget to say something before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I wanted to-- I mean, as far as the anti-Semitism was concerned, of course, you know, like in 19-- in Hungary in 1937, as early as 1932, you know, they came out with some Jewish laws, you know, which was against orthodoxy. Because you know, they didn't want to let them kill kosher, you know, and certain other things. And then of course they started taking away land and properties from Jews. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the '30s. You know, in '32, '33. And then later on, as it progressed, you know, there was more and more taken away, especially from my, my grandfathers. And then the same thing happened in the '40s. And then right after, in 1942 or '43, they started to take away licenses-- you know, like work permits and business permits-- from Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, my father had this little particular factory, you know? And he wasn't able to work or, or do business. The only ones who could do business was probably a few artisans like tailors and, and things like that, you know? And then of course bigger businesses were taken away, you know, for the war effort, you know? So they were taken away from the Jewish owners and were given to, you know, Hungarian overseers, so to speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then of course my father, you know, didn't want to go in. He was able for somehow to buy himself out, not to go into this particular work detail which I mentioned. You know, instead of military duty, they took the Jews to work detail. Of course, he was an Orthodox man, didn't want to cut his beard. And plus the fact that they were taking people to the front and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was able to, you know, go up to the military-- what do you call it? I mean, he was able-- I mean, Hungarian was like his first language. And he was very well-- in other words, he was able to negotiate with them so to buy himself out. And then in the last-- just before the ghetto was started, for a couple of months they were looking for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think after Horthy-- Horthy, Miklos, which was the-- he was the president of Hungary. And when Szalasi, which was the fascist ruler who came right after him, took over, and that was about when the Germans, you know, kind of came into Hungary and sort of took over Hungary. And so my father was sort of in hiding, so he wasn't home. So he wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did he hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a town-- at my uncle's in-- it was a town, [NON-ENGLISH] And that's where he stayed. And that's where he was taken from the ghetto to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's where they found him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wasn't with you guys--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with the family anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that, saying goodbye to your father? Did you know that he was going--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I don't think, you know, they, they realized or they thought, you know, that this thing will come to us, you know? That we'll be taken into ghettos and then deported to Germany. Because, you know, the Russians were very, very close. They were only about maybe 200 kilometers from us in the Carpathian Mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if you know [? May, ?] [? May, ?] which was the Hungarian Jews at that particular time, deportation, that was the D-Day, you know? And of course, the Russians didn't want to move. Otherwise, I mean, if they would've moved-- I don't know if it was purposely or whatever it was-- they would have probably-- could've taken Hungary. And then we could have been saved. So start when-- was it, is it March '43 that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, March '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The Germans sort of, I think, came into Hungary in 19-- in January '43, when they took over, you know? And then took about-- January, February-- in March, March of '44, when they started the ghettos. And they started gathering all the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this was in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in-- well, to preface, I went-- that particular season, right after the high Holidays, you know, I went to a yeshiva, which was-- Opalyi was the name of the yeshiva, you know? And I was there-- let's see-- September, October, November, and December, January. January--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I think February. February was in the yeshiva. And then, for some reason or other, I suppose they realized, you know, that something is gonna happen. So they dissolved the yeshiva. And at that particular time--

speaker: interviewer
text: What town was it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was called Palyi-- Opalyi. It was a little town in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were at a yeshiva. You were no longer with your family. Your mother and younger--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --siblings were still in Satmar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- no, this was already Hungary. From 1944, when the Romanians-- I mean the Romanians relinquished Transylvania and gave it back to the Hungarians, and it became Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Satmar became Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungary. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And suddenly, your mother and the siblings were vulnerable to the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they-- well, they were-- well, everybody was, you know? I mean, it wasn't in particular, or one particular--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But you were no longer in the home. You were living in a yeshiva.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a yeshiva for a couple of months. You know, like I said, three, four months of that particular winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Your father had already been-- had run away to hide, and had been taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. My father hid in this particular town, which is [NON-ENGLISH] at my uncle's home. And he stayed, you know, because he figured at home, they might, you know, look for him. But they wouldn't realize that he is in another town, or where he is. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother had the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother and my elder brother was home in Satmar. You know, in our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did the family get taken? Describe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well anyway, they made a ghetto. You understand? Which was-- in our area, where we lived, they brought in people. You know, like for instance our home-- besides us alone, you know, there was maybe another two or three families, you know, maybe four families-- I don't recall exactly-- you know, brought in to stay with us. You know, so one family lived in one room, a bedroom. The other one lived in another bedroom, and you know. So actually instead of, let's say, seven, eight people, there was, all of a sudden there was about 20 people or so. You know, and each was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother manage that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess everybody, you know, tried to manage the best they can, you know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, their food-- for some reason or other, you know, since we had a farm, you know, we were allotted food. You know, like we were able to get flour and different things, and of course potatoes and things, wood, and so on and so forth, which was prepared months and months before that. You know? So there was food. And then the people brought some food. And of course I'm sure they must have shared some of the food. And this was around-- I remember it was Passover. Passover was still, you know-- of '44, we still-- you know, we celebrated Passover. And right after Passover, the ghetto started. And then of course, you know, it just took about, I don't know, two, three weeks. And they started sending away people. You know, selection, you know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell me about the selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- I, I, I mean, of course, you know, they were stressing, you know, that they--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were-- you mean the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not the Germans. The Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hungarians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarians. There was some Germans which I recall. There were some Germans, you know, who came in, you know, with the Hungarians. But it mainly was the Hungarian. And it wasn't necessarily the military. There was the border-- some border military, which were-- they were called Csendors. Csendor-- Csendor means, you know, the-- csen means quiet. The quiet-- those who had the authority over, you know, keeping quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were mainly in the smaller towns, you know? They took care on the smaller town. Plus, the border, border patrol-- you understand? They were with the military suits or uniforms. And in their hats, they had some feathers and things like that. They were supposed to be the rougher, you know. And then little by little, you know, they emptied-- you know, they emptied-- I think we were a transport just before the last, you know, which take-- took us to Auschwitz. And they took us-- they walked us-- you know, there was a big square which was near, right across from the military installations there, you know? And they surrounded us, you know, with our-- they-- I don't recall what they told us what to take. You know, but everybody carried something-- you know, suitcases and things like that. And then of course they took us to the trains and, and packed us into these cars. You know, the-- You were saying before about selection. Would you tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not necessarily selection. But I mean, they said-- you know, like for instance, different areas. They said well, we are right-- we are taking these and these people from this street or from this area. The people from here will be in this and this particular day will be taken, you know, to work camps or whatever they--

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, you were home from the, from the Opalyi Yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Well, I came home from the February. And so my, my father wrote me, you know, that I should go up to Budapest, you know? And there was a certain rabbi there. They called him the Belzer Rebbe. I don't know if you heard of him. And he-- at that particular time, you know, he was smuggled out somehow from Poland. And he lived in, in Hungary, in Budapest. And of course, you know, somehow or other, they, they made provisions for him to, to go to Israel. So he knew that-- you know, so he wanted me to see him. You know, I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: To see him or to go with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. To see him. To see him, not to go with-- I mean, you needed a certificate in order to go to Israel at that particular time. It wasn't just going. I suppose a lot of people could have gotten somehow. But you know, the rabbis for some reason or other-- Gott vet helfen, Gott vet helfen, you know? And God's help, and nothing is going to happen. You know? So a lot of them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what they were all saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Satmar Rebbe saying that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I necessarily-- I mean, I didn't necessarily hear it, you know? But I mean, people were talking that the rabbi constantly-- I mean, that was through years and years, you know, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Calming people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And of course, you know, they were saying, you know, the Messiah has to come before we could go to Israel. And this is-- I mean, they kept up. Even though he, he was saved, and he was saved to Switzerland. And from Switzerland, they took him to Israel. And then from Israel, he came to New York. But even afterwards when he settled to New York, I mean, and Israel was established, and he was still-- they were very adamant, you know, not to-- you know, to their followers, not to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So there wasn't a particular selection. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I said, they took a group of so many and so many people, I suppose--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family, to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gathered us to the, to the square, you know? And they marched us, you know, to the train, which was quite a considerable march, you know-- I would say at least about four, five kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was, who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was me, my brother, my mother, and, and all the rest of the children. You know? And then of course, all the other people who, who were, you know, selected at that particular day to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, they, you know, they walked us in the middle of the streets. They walked us. And of course, you could realize, you know, how we felt-- you know, taken from your homes, and, well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they walked us-- as I remember, it was a sunny day. And they walked us to the train. And they loaded us in these carts, you know, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Railway.

speaker: interviewee
text: Railway cars. And they, they, they took us, you know, towards Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a small child. Didn't realize what and when. And of course, you know, like for instance in each car there was maybe 40, 50 people, or maybe even more-- you know, children and elderly people and, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get into the same car with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. We-- I mean, they didn't try to separate no families at that particular time. You know, and they kind of herded us, you know? I mean, while we were walking through the town, they weren't trying to be rough, you know? But then, of course, you know, they were forceful. You know, they were forceful. And you know, get in, get in, get in. I mean, I mean, it's kind of fading after 50 years or so, you know. It's-- It's-- you know, I mean, it's not quite as, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help your mother up into the cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she get up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- well, they probably-- I don't recall. But I'm sure that they must have had something, a plank or something, to go up there. You know? So of course, you know, like mothers, I remember my mother, you know, trying to help in the car. You know, help, you know, the younger children, the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't even recall, you know, as far as food. They must have had some, some food. And they did bring up water. And then of course, you know, they had-- in the car there was some provision, you know, to relieve yourselves, you know? What kind of provision?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They must have had some kind of-- I don't exactly recall. They didn't let you out for that matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember how you went.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I don't recall really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the children, the younger children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, younger children-- you know, I mean, children, I mean, we-- Jewish children, for some reason or other, we were sort of-- how shall I say? We were conditioned, you know, in situations like that, not to complain and not to cry or anything. And my youngest sister was five, you know? But I mean, it was a very, very morbid, you know, situation. Nobody was this-- I don't remember or recall discussing anything. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the scene inside the railway car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it took about-- I remember, for some reason or other, I remember-- not exactly, but we must've gone to the-- in the direction of Slovakia. Because I remember a town, you know, like Presov. You know? And I remember, you know, they had rolling hills, you know, with beautiful grass. It was in the springtime.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you could see out. You know, there was some, some-- you know, with bars, bars, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: There were windows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Windows, yeah. Windows with bars. And it was in, sort of in the springtime, so it wasn't really that hot or anything like that, that it wasn't-- it was very uncomfortable. And as far as sleeping, I don't recall. But I mean, I suppose we must've fallen asleep curled up or some-- you know, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have plenty of room?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there wasn't room. I mean, a railroad car is only maybe from the wall to the sofa, and probably this length of the living room. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people do you figure were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I figure there must've been about 50 people. At least 50 people. You know, I mean, children, you know, and-- there were a lot of people were huddled, you know, mainly towards the walls of the place, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I could recall, probably it must've taken about between five to six days, something like that. You know, there was places that they stopped, you know, and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they just gave you water and no food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember they should-- that they give us, gave us any, any food whatsoever. No. I think water. Water. We didn't-- you know, I suppose whatever they took along. Bread, you know, like in Hungary they made these big breads, you know? So it must've been a couple of breads, and maybe whatever else I don't recall, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about the other people in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very well, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, there was families that I knew. But don't forget that there was also not necessarily people from Satmar itself, you know? Because Satmar people, I knew. I would say, at least 60%, 70% I knew. But they brought in-- from all the surrounding areas, they brought in people which we didn't know, you know? And they brought them into the same ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, you know, so then we arrived in Auschwitz. You know, Auschwitz, you know, and then I recall the people who would just hurry you down, you know, from the trains. And I remember the selection. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And me, my brother-- of course, I didn't even know if my sister was selected, you know, to-- or anybody. Just me and my brother. They were separating us. The selection, you know. And of course I don't remember who, really, it was, if it was Mengele or-- of course everybody said it was Mengele. And of course, he always had an assistant. And that's it. And the children and my mother went to another side. And they took us into, to the camp, and you know, went into what they called delousing or whatever they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Delousing, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Delousing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then, you know, we stripped everything. And they gave us clothing. And-- pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. They cut their hair off, you know, and everything. Payos and, you know, with people with beard, everything was-- they sheared everybody off, you know? And they put us into a camp, into a barrack. I don't remember. I think I remember it was Barrack 2, if I remember. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we stayed over there for maybe a week. I remember, if I recall-- well, I think it was the second day of Shovuos that they took us away, you know? I mean, they marched us to the train and they took us, like I said, to, to Plaszow. Did you have any clue as to what happened to your mother and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. We didn't have any, any clue. You know, it's just-- I mean, everybody was talking like in sign language. Maybe the older people, you know, more grown up people who might have spoken Slavic, you understand, and spoke with these people from, you know, from the camps, and they would probably-- but you know, they tried to protect us in a sense, that it didn't come out to the younger people and tell you, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we surmised, you know, that something, something is happening. But I mean, we never heard this, you know? Of course, you know, we knew that there was quite a lot of killing going on in Poland. Polish Jews, you know, were killed. You did know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, but---

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But the specific-- because like I said, you know, people used to talk, you know? And people, some people who ran away from Poland, you know, and told some people. But it wasn't conversation which people, grown up people, would relate to, to smaller, smaller children.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 14 already by then, weren't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was 15.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just turned 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: With Nachmen. Nachmen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was older, two years older. But for some reason or other, he looked younger, you know, because he was a little sickly looking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you together the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were moved from Auschwitz. And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Plaszow. Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were moved to Plaszow. And we, we were put into a block over there. It was called-- you know, to a barrack. Barrack 39, I remember it was. And there was-- 39, you know, sort of was kind of separated from the other barracks. Because this was a transitional people. And the people were sort of towards-- there was a small little-- like a-- little, not a hill, but sort of like a little higher up ground. And there were the-- there was a lot of people, you know, who were there, regular people lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, you know, I could never understand how-- I mean how many, so many people-- because I knew there was about between 15,000 to 19,000 people, that there was that many-- where they were housed, you know? And of course, I remember that there was, on the hill, you know, from the men's barrack, you know, and there was, like, wires, you know, like a gated area. And there was women and children still in that particular camp at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, us they fed, you know, just soup. And they gave us some bread. But I mean, bread was-- you know, at that particular time, I wasn't a big eater. So I don't know. Some other people, you know, probably elderly people-- you know, I remember I ate very, very little, you know, in the beginning. You know, sort of no appetite. I wasn't a big eater anyway at that particular time. But so actually, as far as food is concerned, you know, the starvation business didn't start till later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Plaszow, you had enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? Not enough, but I mean, in Plaszow, you know-- like for instance, later on, I-- you know, the cooks, you know, would sometimes give you coffee. And especially if they gave you some coffee with sugar in it. You know, I was able to, to take some and sell it, you know, and make a few zlotys. And then there was-- they used to bring in bread and salami and things like that. I mean, it wasn't an everyday occurrence. Once in a while, you know, we bought a little extra. So me and my brother would share it. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, you know, I remember I-- we went to work on a detail. And then there was these fellas from-- I think they came from [NON-ENGLISH] or somewhere, from their language. And they were [NON-ENGLISH], you know? They were, you know-- what do you call them? Tanners, who tanned. You know? And they, for some reason-- so he says to me, he says, why don't I give you a couple of candies? You know, try to sell it. You sell it for so many zlotys, you know? So until there was-- so I used to sell it and make a few zlotys, you know, 10, 12 zlotys, you know. And I couldn't-- we couldn't go there all the time, you know? But when I saw him, I knew where he was in camp to give him the money. But then later on, you know, there was, from our hometown, you know, somebody, an overseer, who made him overseer. He realized, you know, that I have candy. He says, give me a candy, give me a candy. So and of course, I was very honest, you know? And I says to him, I says, I don't want any more candy. Because he eats my candy. And I won't be able to pay you back any money. So I stopped that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then of course, then later on, we were working in different details, you know, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Plaszow? What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Plaszow, the Germans were-- first, they took us to details, you know, where we used to clean or dig, dig, you know, like ditches, and carry stones in these little carts, you know, on, on--

speaker: interviewer
text: Wheelbarrows?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, there was some wheelbarrow work, too. But mainly they had, like, those lorries, you know, which you-- what do you call it? On tracks. And we did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember, you know, when-- I mean, of course I didn't necessarily see it. But when we passed [? Goeth ?] You know-- you heard of [? Goeth? ?] The lageralteste, you know, the [? lager-- ?] [? theater. ?] You know, who used to just shoot people. Of course, we knew about it, you know? And I've seen him on his balcony many times, you know, I suppose. I was lucky by-- You did see him in the balcony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I saw him [INAUDIBLE] on the Appellplatz, you know, and different, you know, and different places. So actually, we were kind of afraid, you know? But we didn't have no choice, you know? We used to go by, and head down, you know, so he doesn't see us, you know? I mean, we shouldn't be obvious. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, of course, there was the, the regular Appellplatz, you know, where we used to all come out in the morning and stand in lines, you know, from each barrack, you know? And then different things was going on at that particular time. You know, there was this [NON-ENGLISH] and his wife, who were the lageraltestes, you know, from the-- in other words, they were Jewish, you know? They were the Jewish overseers. And then there was hangings and punishments and all kind of different things. And then later on, you know, they were building some office buildings, you know? And then they used to-- sort of like from grass or something, they were pressed, you know? And they used to use that for wall or partitions. And then we had to plaster it.
